title: Array Transformations and Method Selection

learningObjectives.text: Explain `map` using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `filter` using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `reduce` using a new example and justify the result.
Trace and explain method chaining using a new example and justify the result.
Explain choosing an array method using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns array transformations and method selection from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Data Structures and Functional Techniques** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **`map`** → **`filter`** → **`reduce`** → **Method chaining** → **Choosing an array method**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: `map`
sections.body: Array map transforms every input element into one output element and returns a new array of the same length.

sections.title: `filter`
sections.body: Array filter returns a new array containing only elements whose predicate is truthy.

sections.title: `reduce`
sections.body: Array reduce combines a sequence into one accumulated result. An explicit initial value and clear accumulator shape improve reliability.

sections.title: Method chaining
sections.body: Chaining passes one method's result to the next to form a transformation pipeline. Name intermediate values when the chain becomes hard to inspect.

sections.title: Choosing an array method
sections.body: Choose by output intent: map transforms, filter selects, find returns one match, some/every answer boolean questions, and reduce aggregates.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The pipeline selects, transforms, and aggregates without mutating the input.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Array Transformations and Method Selection connects `map`, `filter`, `reduce`.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Array Transformations and Method Selection in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

choices.label: Apply `map` deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Array Transformations and Method Selection?

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from `map`, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Array Transformations and Method Selection?

choices.label: Use Array Transformations and Method Selection in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

choices.label: Use Array Transformations and Method Selection when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Avoid Array Transformations and Method Selection completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Array Transformations and Method Selection.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
